tests.title: IELTS Listening — Test 01
tests.description: Full Listening MVP test: four sections, 40 questions, static TTS audio, transcript-backed explanations.

sections.title: Section 1 — Community garden booking
sections.instructions: Questions 1-10. Complete the notes. Write ONE WORD AND/OR A NUMBER for each answer.
sections.passage:
Receptionist: Good morning, Riverside Community Garden. How can I help?
Caller: Hello. I would like to book a plot for the spring growing programme.
Receptionist: Certainly. Can I take your surname?
Caller: It is Karim, K-A-R-I-M.
Receptionist: Thank you. And the plot size?
Caller: A half plot should be enough because I am a beginner.
Receptionist: The fee for a half plot is sixty-five pounds for the season.
Caller: That sounds fine. Does the fee include compost?
Receptionist: Yes, compost is included, but you need to bring your own gloves.
Caller: I can do that. Which day is the beginner session?
Receptionist: The beginner session is on Wednesday evening, from six until seven thirty.
Caller: Great. I work nearby at the library, so that is convenient.
Receptionist: We also ask new gardeners to attend one safety talk in March.
Caller: March is fine. Is there a gate code?
Receptionist: Yes, after payment you will receive gate code 4182 by email.
Caller: Could you send it to samira dot karim at mailhub dot com?
Receptionist: Of course. Finally, please arrive through the north entrance because the south gate is being repaired.
Caller: Thank you. I will use the north entrance.

questions.explanation: The caller gives the surname Karim and spells it.
The caller says a half plot is enough.

evidence.quote: A half plot should be enough

questions.explanation: The receptionist says the fee is sixty-five pounds.
Compost is included in the fee.
The caller needs to bring gloves.
The beginner session is on Wednesday evening.
The caller says they work nearby at the library.

evidence.quote: I work nearby at the library

questions.explanation: New gardeners attend a safety talk in March.
The gate code is 4182.
The caller should use the north entrance.

sections.title: Section 2 — Museum orientation
sections.instructions: Questions 11-15. Choose the correct letter A-H. Questions 16-20. Choose the correct answer.
sections.passage:
Guide: Welcome to the Museum of Everyday Design. The information desk is marked A, just inside the glass entrance. The lockers are at B, before you reach the main staircase. The cafe is C, beside the courtyard windows. The temporary exhibition is D, in the long room behind the cafe. The children learning studio is E, opposite the lifts. The lecture theatre is F, at the end of the east corridor. The toilets are G, near the shop. Finally, the design shop is H, next to the exit.
Guide: A few practical points. The museum opened in 2014 after the old factory was restored. Photography is allowed in most rooms, but visitors must not touch the objects. The busiest time is usually Saturday morning. If you prefer a quieter visit, come after three o'clock. Guided tours leave every forty-five minutes. The current special exhibition focuses on kitchen tools, not furniture. Tickets are valid all day, so you may leave and return once.

questions.explanation: The information desk is marked A.

evidence.quote: The information desk is marked A

questions.explanation: The cafe is C.
The temporary exhibition is D.
The lecture theatre is F.
The design shop is H.
The guide says the museum opened in 2014.
Photography is mostly allowed, but touching objects is not.

questions.text: The quietest recommended visiting time is
questions.explanation: The guide recommends coming after three o'clock for a quieter visit.
Tours leave every forty-five minutes.
The current special exhibition focuses on kitchen tools.

sections.title: Section 3 — Student research meeting
sections.instructions: Questions 21-25. Choose A, B, or C. Questions 26-30. Complete the notes. Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS.
sections.passage:
Tutor: Tell me about your research project on commuting habits.
Lena: I think our questionnaire is too general because it asks about transport but not about cost.
Owen: I agree about cost, but my bigger concern is the sample size. We only have twenty responses.
Tutor: You need at least fifty responses if you want the results to mean anything.
Lena: We can collect more at the student centre on Thursday.
Owen: Thursday is fine. I also think the graphs are clear, especially the one comparing bus and bicycle use.
Tutor: The graphs are clear, but the literature review is weak. It needs more recent sources.
Lena: I can add articles about remote working because that changed commuting patterns.
Owen: I will rewrite the methods section. It currently sounds like a diary, not a formal report.
Tutor: Good. In your conclusion, avoid claiming that cars will disappear. Say instead that car use may decline among students if public transport becomes cheaper.
Lena: So our main recommendation should be cheaper public transport.
Tutor: Yes, and mention safer cycle lanes as a secondary recommendation.

questions.explanation: Lena says the questionnaire is too general.

questions.text: The sample size is the main concern.
questions.explanation: Owen says his bigger concern is the sample size.

evidence.quote: my bigger concern is the sample size

questions.text: At least fifty responses are needed.
questions.explanation: The tutor states that at least fifty responses are needed.
Owen says the graphs are clear and the tutor agrees.

evidence.quote: I also think the graphs are clear

questions.explanation: Owen says he will rewrite the methods section.

evidence.quote: I will rewrite the methods section

questions.explanation: The project is about commuting habits.

questions.text: More responses will be collected at the student ____.
questions.explanation: Lena says they can collect more at the student centre.

questions.text: The literature review needs more recent ____.
questions.explanation: The tutor says the literature review needs more recent sources.

questions.text: Lena will add articles about ____ working.
questions.explanation: Lena will add articles about remote working.
The main recommendation should be cheaper public transport.

sections.title: Section 4 — Lecture on urban trees
sections.instructions: Questions 31-40. Complete the lecture notes. Write ONE WORD ONLY for each answer.
sections.passage: Lecturer: Today I will discuss why urban trees are now treated as infrastructure rather than decoration. First, trees reduce heat by shading streets and by releasing water vapour through their leaves. This cooling effect can lower energy demand in nearby buildings. Second, roots slow stormwater, so less rain enters drains at once. Third, trees trap fine particles, although they cannot solve air pollution alone. City planners must choose species carefully. A tree with shallow roots may damage pavements, while a tree with dense pollen may worsen allergies. Maintenance is also essential. Young trees need watering during their first two summers. Without early care, many die before they provide shade. The economic case is becoming stronger. When researchers count lower flood damage, improved health, and higher retail activity, trees often return more money than they cost. However, benefits are not shared equally. Wealthier districts usually have more canopy cover, so new planting should prioritise streets where residents face the greatest heat risk.

questions.text: Urban trees are now treated as ____.
questions.explanation: The lecturer says trees are treated as infrastructure.
Trees reduce heat by shading streets.
The cooling effect can lower energy demand.
Roots slow stormwater.
Trees trap fine particles.
A tree with shallow roots may damage pavements.
Dense pollen may worsen allergies.

questions.text: Young trees need ____ during their first two summers.
questions.explanation: Young trees need watering during their first two summers.

questions.text: Researchers count lower flood damage, improved health, and higher retail ____.
questions.explanation: The lecturer mentions higher retail activity.

questions.text: New planting should prioritise streets with the greatest heat ____.
questions.explanation: Planting should prioritise residents facing the greatest heat risk.
